JS From Zero EP.13: What Is the DOM, Really?
Day thirteen of being reborn into this world. This time I want to talk about the DOM — the Document Object Model.
DOM stands for Document Object Model. In earlier chapters we wrote something called
in a bunch of our code — and that
is exactly the DOM we're talking about here.
The DOM is one of those foundational concepts every web developer has to understand. It looks roughly like this:
The DOM kind of looks like a tree diagram, which is why people also call it the DOM Tree.
So why bother understanding the DOM? Think back to the earlier chapters — whenever we used a selector, we'd write
part represents the very top level of the DOM:
Every layer underneath the DOM has its own meaning. Once you understand the DOM, the whole idea of manipulating it gets a lot clearer.
A DOM Element (or just element) mainly refers to these — everything in the red boxes is an element:
Everything in the red boxes is an element

DOM Text is exactly what it sounds like — the text inside an element. We can change it with
. The way to picture it: we use
to go find the element with the ID
and hand it back to us, then we use the

A DOM Attribute is, well, an attribute. The easiest way to think about an attribute is something like a
to select an attribute and then set or modify it. For example,
would turn the HTML into this:
There's one detail to keep in mind at the end here. If you use
, what you get back is a NodeList — and a NodeList is
an array. It's an array-like object, and it's a live collection. "Live" means it changes along with the content, so it might have fewer methods than a real array.

pairing we talked about in earlier chapters.

Day thirteen of my JavaScript journey. This post is all about the DOM — the Document Object Model. We'll cover what the DOM tree is, what DOM elements, text, and attributes are, and why a NodeList isn't quite an array. It's the foundational concept every web dev needs to get.
